title: Born in Marion County, Oldest of Ten
summary: Magee was born in Marion County, Columbia, Mississippi in 1932, the oldest of ten children of Glessie and O'Reilly Leggett Magee, a sharecropper father and a mother who worked the house and the fields.

title: A Father Who Vowed His Children Would Read
summary: He describes a father who, before he ever married, vowed his children would go to school, who cleared new ground with an axe to buy land, and a self-sufficient farm with no electricity or plumbing where the family raised everything they ate and hauled water up the hill.

title: The Grandfather's Broken Promise, and Tragedy at Alcorn
summary: Magee tells the story of his father's father breaking a promise to let the children attend school, which fixed his father's vow, and his own first great setback when his mother died from childbirth thirty days into his time at Alcorn, leaving him to choose whether to go back.

title: A Diabetic Coma and a Grandmother's House
summary: He recounts his father's diabetic coma during that same school year, the doctor's order to keep working and go on a diet that bought him decades, and how his grandmother across the lane took in and raised four of the children after his mother's death.

part: Work and the Road to College
title: Plowing, Picking Cotton, and Beating His Father
summary: Magee describes his father the trainer, who paid each child a penny a pound to teach them weighing and arithmetic, and the morning he came in late, was scolded, and out of anger beat his own father picking cotton, then beat him every day after.

title: The Homemade Bus and Hand-Me-Down Books
summary: He recalls walking a mile and a half in any weather to catch the homemade bus a relative ran while white children rode a real bus past the house, the books worn five years with other children's names in them, and a community that helped keep the education-minded family on the right path.

title: Choosing Alcorn, and the Nickname KT
summary: Magee explains choosing the rural agricultural Alcorn over a city school because he feared the nightlife, working jobs to pay his room and board, and learning, from a garbage-detail nickname, how not to react to what was said to get a rise out of him.

title: Drafted to Germany, and a Marriage
summary: Deferred through college by an arrangement with the draft board, Magee was drafted after graduating, married a woman he met in Marion County during a furlough, and shipped to Germany for two years while a home was purchased for them back in Hattiesburg.

title: A Teaching Career, and the Federal Government
summary: He traces his career teaching across Mississippi, at Wiggins, Prentiss Institute, Eureka, Lillie Burney, and Rowan, before moving to Mobile in 1974 for a federal food-stamp job he held until retirement, working a compressed week to be home weekends.

title: Two Houses, and the Back Porch
summary: Magee describes how the bond between his sharecropping family and the white family they worked for could feel close, sharing the warmer house in winter, yet at that family's table the Magees ate on the back porch, a line that vanished only inside their own home.

title: What You Learn by Watching
summary: He recalls being hurried out the back of a pool hall, a car that nearly hit him on the street, and learning from his parents' silent actions which places not to go and which things not to say, following good leaders.

title: What Do You Want, Boy
summary: After buying a home in 1956 and paying the two-dollar poll tax, Magee went to register, met a deputy clerk who said the registrar was out, then a registrar who demanded who sent him, called him boy, and told him to wait, all without explanation.

title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
summary: He recounts the literacy test and constitution interpretation he treated as a cat-and-mouse game, his wife's repeated attempts, and the federal Fifth Circuit case in which her application was set beside that of a white truck driver who could not read yet was passed, helping hold the registrar in contempt.

title: J.C. Fairley and a Church That Turned Them Away
summary: Magee traces his NAACP connection through branch president J.C. Fairley, a fellow churchgoer, and the army experience that sensitized him, a white soldier turned away with him from a Virginia church, gross to him in a house of worship, which led most of his small congregation to join.

title: Sensitivity Training for Freedom Summer
summary: He describes the work he did most during Freedom Summer, sensitivity training for Northern youngsters who did not know the local culture, warning them what words and gestures would set people off and where the danger spots were, while churches housed and fed the volunteers.

title: The Biracial Education Committee
summary: Magee recounts the biracial committee working on a school-desegregation strategy, how the Black members met first to role-play the questions, and the volatile meetings with Superintendent Spinks and a finger-pointing principal whom the committee chair met point for point.

title: Harassment and a Pickup-Truck Chase
summary: He describes the harassment and terror that the word terrorist only later named, a threatening call to his wife during the trial, a man trying to shoulder him in the courthouse hallway, and the night a pickup truck chased him through Hattiesburg until he outmaneuvered it.

title: Smuggling Dr. King, and the False Notion of Freedom
summary: Magee notes he missed the night Dr. King was smuggled into town, frames his desegregation hopes as better supplies rather than rubbing shoulders, and presses his recurring theme that a freedom you do not struggle for is easily lost and carries a huge responsibility.

title: Poverty Has No Eyes
summary: Drawing on his federal work visiting homes that received benefits, Magee reflects that poverty has no eyes and that comfort can dull the incentive to do better, illustrated with the cell phone his wife left at a gas station, a parable of unfamiliar technology.

title: N.R. Burger's Buffer and the Rowan Walkout
summary: He recalls how city teachers kept their jobs while county teachers known to be in the NAACP were dismissed, the principal N.R. Burger acting as a buffer, the 1969 student walkout and march from Rowan he monitored, and why he took a non-teaching job since teachers had no tenure.

title: Witness for Clyde Kennard
summary: Magee and his wife were subpoenaed to Aberdeen in the bitter cold to witness that Clyde Kennard, dying at Parchman, was still alive, both going despite the risk, brought in by the Black attorney R. Jess Brown to satisfy a court order to prove him living.

title: Vernon Dahmer, a Burning Cross, and Sam Bowers
summary: He remembers Vernon Dahmer through sons who were his classmates, standing watch near the highway after Dahmer's house was burned, the chemical cross sprayed on his own lawn while he kept a rifle by the door, and the eventual conviction of Sam Bowers.

part: Legacy and the Work That Remains
title: Education, Education, Education
summary: Magee says the legacy to remember is the courage to keep on, that three words, education, education, and education, would solve the country's ills, and that the stories must be told because they were left out of the history books even though the people were history.

title: Building Housing and a Head Start Agency
summary: He describes the Bread Association for civic improvement that built community housing later lost to Katrina, and how a biracial committee he helped form outmaneuvered a senator's paper plan to win Forrest County its own community-action agency and Head Start.

title: The University, and Preventing a Ferguson
summary: Magee recounts his relationship with the once-segregated University of Southern Mississippi and its Black president, mediating campus conflicts, and the NAACP's proactive work to keep the city from becoming a Ferguson, since here both an officer and the victim bled into the same red soil.

title: A Great-Uncle Lynched, and Restorative Justice
summary: Without bitterness, Magee closes on a great-uncle lynched on Richburg Road in 1929 with no one ever brought to trial, his interest in the restorative-justice efforts a few communities are attempting, and his sense that it could just as easily have been him, honored simply to be alive and still contributing.
